content.overview:
Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral infection caused by the dengue virus (DENV), transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes. It is one of the most rapidly spreading vector-borne diseases globally. India records among the highest dengue burden in Southeast Asia, with cases peaking during and after monsoon season (July–November). Most infections are mild, but a subset progresses to severe dengue (dengue haemorrhagic fever), which can be fatal if not treated promptly.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for health awareness only. Do not self-diagnose or self-medicate. Dengue can deteriorate rapidly — always consult a qualified healthcare provider if you suspect dengue, especially if warning signs are present.
types: Mild Dengue Fever: High fever with pain; self-limiting in most cases.
Dengue with Warning Signs: Abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, fluid accumulation — requires close medical monitoring.
Severe Dengue (Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Dengue Shock Syndrome): Severe plasma leaking, severe bleeding, and organ impairment. Medical emergency.
symptoms: Classic symptoms (appear 4–10 days after infective mosquito bite):
Severe muscle and joint pain ("breakbone fever")
Characteristic skin rash (appears 2–5 days after fever onset)
warning_signs: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Living in or travelling to tropical/subtropical regions
Monsoon and post-monsoon season (stagnant water breeding sites)
Previous dengue infection (second infection with different serotype increases severe dengue risk)
Poor sanitation and open water storage
prevention: Use mosquito repellents (DEET-based or picaridin-based) on exposed skin
Wear long-sleeved clothing, especially during early morning and evening
Use mosquito nets (especially for infants, elderly, and pregnant women)
Empty, clean, or cover water containers (coolers, flower pots, tyres, buckets) at least once a week
Ensure proper disposal of waste and containers that collect rainwater
Use larvicides in water storage containers where emptying is not possible
Dengvaxia (CYD-TDV) vaccine is available but recommended only for individuals with prior confirmed dengue infection. Consult a doctor.
environmental_control: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
vaccination: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: NS1 antigen test: Most useful in first 5 days of fever
Dengue IgM/IgG antibody test: Useful after day 5
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Monitors platelet and white cell count
PCR test: Definitive but less commonly used in field settings
treatment_awareness_only: No specific antiviral treatment exists. Management is supportive:
Adequate rest and hydration (oral rehydration salts recommended)
Avoid NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen) — they increase bleeding risk
Hospitalisation required for severe dengue or warning signs
Platelet transfusion only under medical supervision
Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Fever lasting more than 2 days, especially with severe body pain
Any of the warning signs listed above
Fever in young children, elderly, or pregnant women
Known dengue outbreak in your area
india_specific_context: Dengue is endemic across India; highest burden in Delhi, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh
National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP) under MoHFW monitors and responds to dengue outbreaks
Most government hospitals provide free dengue testing during outbreaks
Helpline: State health department helplines; NVBDCP: nvbdcp.gov.in
